EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of summer heating and high low level moisture content provided the necessary conditions to create a highly unstable environment across the Southern Appalachian region. The instability and more than adequate upper level west to northwesterly flow allowed an mesoscale convective system to remain quite strong as it moved east across the area. Downburst winds in some areas were in the 60 to 70 mile an hour range with speeds as high as 80 miles an hour estimated. Trees were downed across a broad area and resulted in the deaths of three people.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down in the Greenback area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly gradient winds up to 50 mph affected the Nashville metro during the afternoon hours on April 6. A few trees were reportedly blown down across Davidson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly gradient winds affected the Nashville metro during the afternoon hours on April 6. A peak wind gust of 49 mph (43 knots) was measured at the Nashville International Airport ASOS at 145 PM CDT. A few trees were blown down across Davidson County, including a tree blown down in front of the Blair School of Music on the Vanderbilt University campus, and a large tree blown down in the yard of a home in Hermitage.

A band of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in middle Tennessee and dropped south-southeast into southern middle Tennessee during the late afternoon and early evening hours.  The gust fronts from these storms produced widespread winds of 35-50 mph.  A few more isolated locations likely received winds close to 60 mph.  Numerous reports of trees and tree limbs being knocked down were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EMA reports multiple trees knocked down in Lynchburg.

Numerous reports of wind damage were received across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Tspotter report and photos showed the Cedar Creek Marina Dock was damaged and several trees were blown down nearby.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tspotter photo showed a tree uprooted in the backyard of a home on the Indian Lake Peninsula in Hendersonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system moved northeast from the Lower Mississippi Valley across the Southern Appalachians with a modified Arctic air mass in place prior to the system's arrival.  Temperatures were cold enough in this air mass that much of the precipitation that fell across the northern Cumberland Plateau, southwest Virginia, and extreme northeast Tennessee was in the form of snow. Temperatures warmed above freezing for a few hours across much of the remainder of the Great Valley as the surface low approached the valley. However, once the pressure center shifted east into the Carolinas, the rain changed to snow. Winter storm warning criteria was met basically north of the Interstate 40 corridor with amounts ranging from around 8 to 12 inches of snow across the northern Cumberland Plateau across northeast Tennessee into Southwest Virginia. In some higher terrain areas, amounts topped out around 15 to 16 inches across Southwest Virginia with about two feet in the High Knob region. Snowfall totals were closer to 4 to 8 inches just south of this heavier band of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 7 inches was recorded at White Oak.

Temperatures were cold enough in this air mass that much of the precipitation that fell across the northern Cumberland Plateau, southwest Virginia, and extreme northeast Tennessee was in the form of snow. Temperatures warmed above freezing for a few hours across much of the remainder of the Great Valley as the surface low approached the valley. However, once the pressure center shifted east into the Carolinas, the rain changed to snow. Winter storm warning criteria was met basically north of the Interstate 40 corridor with amounts ranging from around 8 to 12 inches of snow across the northern Cumberland Plateau across northeast Tennessee into Southwest Virginia. In some higher terrain areas, amounts topped out around 15 to 16 inches across Southwest Virginia with about two feet in the High Knob region. Snowfall totals were closer to 4 to 8 inches just south of this heavier band of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 7 inches was reported at Leesburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass moved over the Southern Appalachian region earlier in the week and a northerly flow maintained a rather frigid low level atmosphere through the middle part of the week.  Moderate to heavy snowfall occurred in an area along interstate 40 and points north across the Cumberland Plateau, Northeast Tennessee, and Southwest Virginia during the afternoon through early evening hours. Snowfall amounts were generally in the 3 to 5 inch range, although some higher totals were seen on the Cumberland Plateau and across parts of Northeast Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow was reported at Oak Ridge.

Temperatures were cold enough in this air mass that much of the precipitation that fell across the northern Cumberland Plateau, southwest Virginia, and extreme northeast Tennessee was in the form of snow. Temperatures warmed above freezing for a few hours across much of the remainder of the Great Valley as the surface low approached the valley. However, once the pressure center shifted east into the Carolinas, the rain changed to snow. Winter storm warning criteria was met basically north of the Interstate 40 corridor with amounts ranging from around 8 to 12 inches of snow across the northern Cumberland Plateau across northeast Tennessee into Southwest Virginia. In some higher terrain areas, amounts topped out around 15 to 16 inches across Southwest Virginia with about two feet in the High Knob region. Snowfall totals were closer to 4 to 8 inches just south of this heavier band of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 7 inches was reported at Kingsport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass moved over the Southern Appalachian region earlier in the week and a northerly flow maintained a rather frigid low level atmosphere through the middle part of the week.  Moderate to heavy snowfall occurred in an area along interstate 40 and points north across the Cumberland Plateau, Northeast Tennessee, and Southwest Virginia during the afternoon through early evening hours. Snowfall amounts were generally in the 3 to 5 inch range, although some higher totals were seen on the Cumberland Plateau and across parts of Northeast Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow was reported two miles southwest of Limestone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong but fast moving winter storm pushed rapidly southeast across Middle Tennessee during the morning hours on January 20. Locations along and south of a line from Waverly to Spencer received a mix of rain and freezing rain with some light ice accumulations. A more substantial mix of light sleet, freezing rain, and snow occurred north of that line, and portions of the Upper Cumberland near the Kentucky border received as much as 4 inches of snow. Due to temperatures well below freezing across much of the area, many roadways were very hazardous or closed due to ice and snow, and nearly all school systems in Middle Tennessee were closed through the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 2 to 4 inches fell across Clay County. Emergency management reported 4 inches of snow in Pea Ridge, while CoCoRaHS station Red Boiling Springs 6.7 NNE measured 2.8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved into the Mid-South around midnight on January 22nd, 2016. Precipitation began in the form of freezing rain and quickly changed over to snow by the early morning hours of January 22nd, 2016. The snow tapered off to flurries during the afternoon hours. Total snow accumulations ranged from four to nine inches north and west of a Ripley to Camden line. One to three inches of snow fell from Ripley southward to Millington. Elsewhere across West Tennessee, accumulations were an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to an inch of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level low pressure system moved across the Tennessee Valley on Friday, January 22, 2016. Light to moderate rain developed ahead of this system and spread across Middle Tennessee late in the day on Thursday, January 21. Temperatures below freezing north of I-40 lead to this rain falling as freezing rain, with ice accumulations up to 1/3 in the counties near the Kentucky border leading to numerous vehicle accidents and road closures. As the upper low approached and moved directly over Middle Tennessee during the morning on January 22, the rain and freezing rain began mixing with sleet and eventually changed to all snow from northwest to southeast across the region. Snow was heavy at times in many areas along and north of I-40, falling at up to 2 inches per hour during the morning and afternoon on January 22. Snow gradually ended from west to east across Middle Tennessee from the evening hours into Saturday, January 23, although light snow and flurries affected many areas even into the late evening on January 23.||When all was said and done, snow had fallen across every county in Middle Tennessee, with a large range of snowfall totals from just a trace in southwest Wayne County up to an unofficial 13.5 inches in Macon County.  In addition, much of the Nashville metro area received anywhere from 5 to 10 inches of snow, with 8 inches officially falling at the Nashville International Airport and 7.5 inches at the NWS Nashville office. The snow total of 8 inches at Nashville International Airport set a daily highest snowfall record for January 22. This also made the January 22-23, 2016 snowstorm the largest snowfall in Nashville in 28 years. Many other locations received their highest snow totals since the February 3-4, 1998 snow nearly 20 years prior. The widespread heavy snowfall resulted in numerous interstate and road closures, and nearly all Middle Tennessee school systems were closed for up to 1 week after the snowstorm due to the slow melt of the ice and snow covered roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals between 5 and 8 inches were measured across Cheatham County. A COOP observer at Cheatham Dam measured 8 inches of snow, and CoCoRaHS station Kingston Springs 0.3 ENE measured 5.5 inches of snow.

Due to temperatures remaining below freezing, ice accumulated on all surfaces including trees, power lines, and roads. Dozens of vehicle accidents were reported across the county, and I-24 was closed near the Kentucky border due to ice. Several power lines were also knocked down resulting in scattered power outages.||Freezing rain changed to snow around 6am on January 22, with snow totals between 5 and 8 inches measured across Montgomery County. A COOP observer in Clarksville measured 5 inches of snow, while a Facebook report indicated 8 inches of snow fell near Exit 1 on I-24 around 5 miles NNE of Clarksville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level low pressure system moved across the Tennessee Valley on Friday, January 22, 2016. Light to moderate rain developed ahead of this system and spread across Middle Tennessee late in the day on Thursday, January 21. Temperatures below freezing north of I-40 lead to this rain falling as freezing rain, with ice accumulations up to 1/3 in the counties near the Kentucky border leading to numerous vehicle accidents and road closures. As the upper low approached and moved directly over Middle Tennessee during the morning on January 22, the rain and freezing rain began mixing with sleet and eventually changed to all snow from northwest to southeast across the region. Snow was heavy at times in many areas along and north of I-40, falling at up to 2 inches per hour during the morning and afternoon on January 22. Snow gradually ended from west to east across Middle Tennessee from the evening hours into Saturday, January 23, although light snow and flurries affected many areas even into the late evening on January 23.||When all was said and done, snow had fallen across every county in Middle Tennessee, with a large range of snowfall totals from just a trace in southwest Wayne County up to an unofficial 13.5 inches in Macon County.  In addition, much of the Nashville metro area received anywhere from 5 to 10 inches of snow, with 8 inches officially falling at the Nashville International Airport and 7.5 inches at the NWS Nashville office. The snow total of 8 inches at Nashville International Airport set a daily highest snowfall record for January 22. This also made the January 22-23, 2016 snowstorm the largest snowfall in Nashville in 28 years. Many other locations received their highest snow totals since the February 3-4, 1998 snow nearly 20 years prior. The widespread heavy snowfall resulted in numerous interstate and road closures, and nearly all Middle Tennessee school systems were closed for up to 1 week after the snowstorm due to the slow melt of the ice and snow covered roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain amounts up to 1/4 of an inch fell across Robertson County from the late evening hours on January 21 to around 6am on January 22. Due to temperatures remaining below freezing, ice accumulated on all surfaces including trees, power lines, and roads. Dozens of vehicle accidents were reported across the county, and I-65 was closed due to ice. Several power lines were also knocked down resulting in scattered power outages.||Freezing rain changed to snow around 6am on January 22, with snow totals between 7 and 10 inches measured across Robertson County. A COOP observer at Springfield Experimental Station measured 8 inches of snow, while a NWS employee in White House measured 10 inches of snow. CoCoRaHS station Cedar Hill 2.6 N measured 7 inches of snow, while CoCoRaHS station Greenbrier 1.4 N measured 9.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong but fast moving winter storm pushed rapidly southeast across Middle Tennessee during the morning hours on January 20. Locations along and south of a line from Waverly to Spencer received a mix of rain and freezing rain with some light ice accumulations. A more substantial mix of light sleet, freezing rain, and snow occurred north of that line, and portions of the Upper Cumberland near the Kentucky border received as much as 4 inches of snow. Due to temperatures well below freezing across much of the area, many roadways were very hazardous or closed due to ice and snow, and nearly all school systems in Middle Tennessee were closed through the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of light sleet, freezing rain, and up to 2.5 inches of snow fell across Putnam County. A COOP observer in Monterey measured 2.5 inches of snow. CoCoRaHS station Cookeville 3.3 SSW measured 1.0 inch of snow, while CoCoRaHS station Monterey 3.9 W measured 0.8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast out of the Lower Mississippi Valley through the Upper Ohio Valley dragging a cold front through the Southern Appalachians. The strong wind field associated with the deep low pressure system generated numerous downed trees across the region. Some convective gusts also developed with a few thunderstorms during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 78 mph wind gust was measured at the Camp Creek wind tower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system moved northeast from the Lower Mississippi Valley across the Southern Appalachians with a modified Arctic air mass in place prior to the system's arrival.  Temperatures were cold enough in this air mass that much of the precipitation that fell across the northern Cumberland Plateau, southwest Virginia, and extreme northeast Tennessee was in the form of snow. Temperatures warmed above freezing for a few hours across much of the remainder of the Great Valley as the surface low approached the valley. However, once the pressure center shifted east into the Carolinas, the rain changed to snow. Winter storm warning criteria was met basically north of the Interstate 40 corridor with amounts ranging from around 8 to 12 inches of snow across the northern Cumberland Plateau across northeast Tennessee into Southwest Virginia. In some higher terrain areas, amounts topped out around 15 to 16 inches across Southwest Virginia with about two feet in the High Knob region. Snowfall totals were closer to 4 to 8 inches just south of this heavier band of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 7 inches was recorded at La Follette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few pulse severe thunderstorms developed across northeastern parts of the region including Southwest Virginia and Northeast Tennessee at mid afternoon. Following this development, a more organized line of storms that formed along an outflow boundary that moved out of South Central Kentucky and Middle Tennessee onto the Cumberland Plateau and continued southeast across much of Central East and Southeast Tennessee.|The mode of severe weather was straight line wind which ended up downing numerous trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down in Greeneville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level low pressure system moved across the Tennessee Valley on Friday, January 22, 2016. Light to moderate rain developed ahead of this system and spread across Middle Tennessee late in the day on Thursday, January 21. Temperatures below freezing north of I-40 lead to this rain falling as freezing rain, with ice accumulations up to 1/3 in the counties near the Kentucky border leading to numerous vehicle accidents and road closures. As the upper low approached and moved directly over Middle Tennessee during the morning on January 22, the rain and freezing rain began mixing with sleet and eventually changed to all snow from northwest to southeast across the region. Snow was heavy at times in many areas along and north of I-40, falling at up to 2 inches per hour during the morning and afternoon on January 22. Snow gradually ended from west to east across Middle Tennessee from the evening hours into Saturday, January 23, although light snow and flurries affected many areas even into the late evening on January 23.||When all was said and done, snow had fallen across every county in Middle Tennessee, with a large range of snowfall totals from just a trace in southwest Wayne County up to an unofficial 13.5 inches in Macon County.  In addition, much of the Nashville metro area received anywhere from 5 to 10 inches of snow, with 8 inches officially falling at the Nashville International Airport and 7.5 inches at the NWS Nashville office. The snow total of 8 inches at Nashville International Airport set a daily highest snowfall record for January 22. This also made the January 22-23, 2016 snowstorm the largest snowfall in Nashville in 28 years. Many other locations received their highest snow totals since the February 3-4, 1998 snow nearly 20 years prior. The widespread heavy snowfall resulted in numerous interstate and road closures, and nearly all Middle Tennessee school systems were closed for up to 1 week after the snowstorm due to the slow melt of the ice and snow covered roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain amounts up to 1/4 of an inch fell across Stewart County from the late evening hours on January 21 to around 5am on January 22. Due to temperatures remaining below freezing, ice accumulated on all surfaces including trees, power lines, and roads. A few power lines were knocked down resulting in scattered power outages.||Freezing rain changed to snow around 6am on January 22, with snow totals around 5 inches reported across Stewart County. Emergency management reported 5 inches of snow in Dover, while CoCoRaHS station Dover 7.8 NNE measured 4.9 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong but fast moving winter storm pushed rapidly southeast across Middle Tennessee during the morning hours on January 20. Locations along and south of a line from Waverly to Spencer received a mix of rain and freezing rain with some light ice accumulations. A more substantial mix of light sleet, freezing rain, and snow occurred north of that line, and portions of the Upper Cumberland near the Kentucky border received as much as 4 inches of snow. Due to temperatures well below freezing across much of the area, many roadways were very hazardous or closed due to ice and snow, and nearly all school systems in Middle Tennessee were closed through the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain up to 1/10 of an inch fell across Humphreys County. Media reports indicated parts of I-40 south of Waverly were closed due to ice on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the Southern Plains toward the Mid-Mississippi Valley while a cold front pushed east toward the Mid-South on February 2, 2016. The airmass across the Mid-South was moist and unstable ahead of the front. A couple rounds of showers and thunderstorms occurred. The first round produced heavy rain and some flash flooding in the Memphis Metropolitan area around noon. Another round of showers and thunderstorms developed along the cold front as it moved through the region during the late afternoon. A weak tornado occurred in Crockett County as this round moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Downtown Memphis. Interstate 40 was flooded at Danny Thomas Boulevard.

Snow gradually ended from west to east across Middle Tennessee from the evening hours into Saturday, January 23, although light snow and flurries affected many areas even into the late evening on January 23.||When all was said and done, snow had fallen across every county in Middle Tennessee, with a large range of snowfall totals from just a trace in southwest Wayne County up to an unofficial 13.5 inches in Macon County.  In addition, much of the Nashville metro area received anywhere from 5 to 10 inches of snow, with 8 inches officially falling at the Nashville International Airport and 7.5 inches at the NWS Nashville office. The snow total of 8 inches at Nashville International Airport set a daily highest snowfall record for January 22. This also made the January 22-23, 2016 snowstorm the largest snowfall in Nashville in 28 years. Many other locations received their highest snow totals since the February 3-4, 1998 snow nearly 20 years prior. The widespread heavy snowfall resulted in numerous interstate and road closures, and nearly all Middle Tennessee school systems were closed for up to 1 week after the snowstorm due to the slow melt of the ice and snow covered roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals up to 2 inches were measured across Perry County. CoCoRaHS station Linden 7.9 NNW measured 1 inch of snow, and a COOP observer measured 2 inches of snow in Lobelville. The public reported a dusting of snow along the Tennessee River west of Linden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the Southern Plains toward the Mid-Mississippi Valley while a cold front pushed east toward the Mid-South on February 2, 2016. The airmass across the Mid-South was moist and unstable ahead of the front. A couple rounds of showers and thunderstorms occurred. The first round produced heavy rain and some flash flooding in the Memphis Metropolitan area around noon. Another round of showers and thunderstorms developed along the cold front as it moved through the region during the late afternoon. A weak tornado occurred in Crockett County as this round moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado snapped or uprooted several trees southwest of, and at the Crockett County Golf Club. It caused roof damage to a mobile home and a permanent structure just south of Highway 412. Two tractor trailers were overturned on Highway 412, with one driver injured. North of Highway 412, the tornado damaged several storage sheds and caused roof damage to several homes. The tornado then crossed Highway 88 and caused roof damage to several buildings at the Crockett County High School. The tornado damaged additional storage sheds and a home roof on the east side of North Cavalier Drive before dissipating. Peak estimated wind was 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front stretched across Northeast Arkansas into Northwest Mississippi on February 14, 2016. A surface low developed along the stalled front and moved eastward. Precipitation developed along and north of the stalled front during the day ahead of the surface low. Temperatures were below freezing north of the front. As a result, precipitation fell in the form of freezing rain and sleet. A tenth to a quarter inch of ice and sleet accumulated north of a Dyersburg to Jackson, Tennessee line. Roads became hazardous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a two-tenths of an inch of ice fell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of summer heating and high low level moisture content provided the necessary conditions to create a highly unstable environment across the Southern Appalachian region. The instability and more than adequate upper level west to northwesterly flow allowed an mesoscale convective system to remain quite strong as it moved east across the area. Downburst winds in some areas were in the 60 to 70 mile an hour range with speeds as high as 80 miles an hour estimated. Trees were downed across a broad area and resulted in the deaths of three people.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported at Loudon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through portions of West Tennessee during the early morning hours of January 20th, 2016. Light to moderate freezing rain fell in association with the disturbance. As a result, a light glaze of a tenth or an inch or less accumulated on elevated surfaces and bridges. In addition, freezing rain changed over to sleet and eventually to all snow near the Kentucky Border. Accumulations of 1-2 inches occurred across Obion, Weakley, and Henry Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than a tenth of an inch of ice accumulated.

Light to moderate freezing rain fell in association with the disturbance. As a result, a light glaze of a tenth or an inch or less accumulated on elevated surfaces and bridges. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front stretched across Northeast Arkansas into Northwest Mississippi on February 14, 2016. A surface low developed along the stalled front and moved eastward. Precipitation developed along and north of the stalled front during the day ahead of the surface low. Temperatures were below freezing north of the front. As a result, precipitation fell in the form of freezing rain and sleet. A tenth to a quarter inch of ice and sleet accumulated north of a Dyersburg to Jackson, Tennessee line. Roads became hazardous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a two-tenths of an inch of ice and sleet fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of summer heating and high low level moisture content provided the necessary conditions to create a highly unstable environment across the Southern Appalachian region. The instability and more than adequate upper level west to northwesterly flow allowed an mesoscale convective system to remain quite strong as it moved east across the area. Downburst winds in some areas were in the 60 to 70 mile an hour range with speeds as high as 80 miles an hour estimated. Trees were downed across a broad area and resulted in the deaths of three people.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down one mile northeast of Clairfield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were reported down along Blades Road one mile northeast of Clairfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few pulse severe thunderstorms developed across northeastern parts of the region including Southwest Virginia and Northeast Tennessee at mid afternoon. Following this development, a more organized line of storms that formed along an outflow boundary that moved out of South Central Kentucky and Middle Tennessee onto the Cumberland Plateau and continued southeast across much of Central East and Southeast Tennessee.|The mode of severe weather was straight line wind which ended up downing numerous trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down in southern Meigs County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong but fast moving winter storm pushed rapidly southeast across Middle Tennessee during the morning hours on January 20. Locations along and south of a line from Waverly to Spencer received a mix of rain and freezing rain with some light ice accumulations. A more substantial mix of light sleet, freezing rain, and snow occurred north of that line, and portions of the Upper Cumberland near the Kentucky border received as much as 4 inches of snow. Due to temperatures well below freezing across much of the area, many roadways were very hazardous or closed due to ice and snow, and nearly all school systems in Middle Tennessee were closed through the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 2 to 4 inches fell across Pickett County. CoCoRaHS station Byrdstown 2.8 SSW measured 3.1 inches of snow, while a COOP observer in Byrdstown measured 2.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level low pressure system moved across the Tennessee Valley on Friday, January 22, 2016. Light to moderate rain developed ahead of this system and spread across Middle Tennessee late in the day on Thursday, January 21. Temperatures below freezing north of I-40 lead to this rain falling as freezing rain, with ice accumulations up to 1/3 in the counties near the Kentucky border leading to numerous vehicle accidents and road closures. As the upper low approached and moved directly over Middle Tennessee during the morning on January 22, the rain and freezing rain began mixing with sleet and eventually changed to all snow from northwest to southeast across the region. Snow was heavy at times in many areas along and north of I-40, falling at up to 2 inches per hour during the morning and afternoon on January 22. Snow gradually ended from west to east across Middle Tennessee from the evening hours into Saturday, January 23, although light snow and flurries affected many areas even into the late evening on January 23.||When all was said and done, snow had fallen across every county in Middle Tennessee, with a large range of snowfall totals from just a trace in southwest Wayne County up to an unofficial 13.5 inches in Macon County.  In addition, much of the Nashville metro area received anywhere from 5 to 10 inches of snow, with 8 inches officially falling at the Nashville International Airport and 7.5 inches at the NWS Nashville office. The snow total of 8 inches at Nashville International Airport set a daily highest snowfall record for January 22. This also made the January 22-23, 2016 snowstorm the largest snowfall in Nashville in 28 years. Many other locations received their highest snow totals since the February 3-4, 1998 snow nearly 20 years prior. The widespread heavy snowfall resulted in numerous interstate and road closures, and nearly all Middle Tennessee school systems were closed for up to 1 week after the snowstorm due to the slow melt of the ice and snow covered roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals between 3 and 6 inches were measured across Cumberland County. A COOP observer at Crossville Experimental Station measured 5.3 inches of snow, while a trained spotter 8 miles east of Monterey measured 5 inches of snow. CoCoRaHS station Crossville 4.7 S measured 4.1 inches of snow, and CoCoRaHS station Crossville 6.9 S measured 3.1 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved into the Mid-South around midnight on January 22nd, 2016. Precipitation began in the form of freezing rain and quickly changed over to snow by the early morning hours of January 22nd, 2016. The snow tapered off to flurries during the afternoon hours. Total snow accumulations ranged from four to nine inches north and west of a Ripley to Camden line. One to three inches of snow fell from Ripley southward to Millington. Elsewhere across West Tennessee, accumulations were an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to nine inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing air spilled south through the Eastern United States for a two day period of mainly orographic snowfall as several shorter wavelength systems dropped southeast out of the Northern Plains and Great Lakes. The snow accumulated to a depth of three to five inches on average however, some greater snowfall totals occurred primarily in the highest terrain across Southwest Virginia and in the Smoky Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three miles south of Unicoi at Sam's Gap, a three inch snowfall accumulation was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain spread across Middle Tennessee on January 9, with the rain changing to light snow across northwest Middle Tennessee and on the Cumberland Plateau from the late evening hours on January 9 into the early morning hours on January 10. A few reports of 1/2 to just over 1 inch of snow were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS station McMinnville 8.5 ESE measured 1.2 of snow in the early morning hours on January 10.

As the upper low approached and moved directly over Middle Tennessee during the morning on January 22, the rain and freezing rain began mixing with sleet and eventually changed to all snow from northwest to southeast across the region. Snow was heavy at times in many areas along and north of I-40, falling at up to 2 inches per hour during the morning and afternoon on January 22. Snow gradually ended from west to east across Middle Tennessee from the evening hours into Saturday, January 23, although light snow and flurries affected many areas even into the late evening on January 23.||When all was said and done, snow had fallen across every county in Middle Tennessee, with a large range of snowfall totals from just a trace in southwest Wayne County up to an unofficial 13.5 inches in Macon County.  In addition, much of the Nashville metro area received anywhere from 5 to 10 inches of snow, with 8 inches officially falling at the Nashville International Airport and 7.5 inches at the NWS Nashville office. The snow total of 8 inches at Nashville International Airport set a daily highest snowfall record for January 22. This also made the January 22-23, 2016 snowstorm the largest snowfall in Nashville in 28 years. Many other locations received their highest snow totals since the February 3-4, 1998 snow nearly 20 years prior. The widespread heavy snowfall resulted in numerous interstate and road closures, and nearly all Middle Tennessee school systems were closed for up to 1 week after the snowstorm due to the slow melt of the ice and snow covered roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals between 5 and 10 inches were measured across Davidson County. A COOP observer in Joelton measured 10 inches of snow, while the official observer at Nashville International Airport measured 8 inches of snow. An NWS employee 1 mile east of Hermitage measured 6.1 inches of snow, CoCoRaHS station Antioch 4.3 ENE measured 6 inches of snow, and CoCoRaHS station Belle Meade 5.3 WSW measured 5.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved into the Mid-South around midnight on January 22nd, 2016. Precipitation began in the form of freezing rain and quickly changed over to snow by the early morning hours of January 22nd, 2016. The snow tapered off to flurries during the afternoon hours. Total snow accumulations ranged from four to nine inches north and west of a Ripley to Camden line. One to three inches of snow fell from Ripley southward to Millington. Elsewhere across West Tennessee, accumulations were an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing air spilled south through the Eastern United States for a two day period of mainly orographic snowfall as several shorter wavelength systems dropped southeast out of the Northern Plains and Great Lakes. The snow accumulated to a depth of three to five inches on average however, some greater snowfall totals occurred primarily in the highest terrain across Southwest Virginia and in the Smoky Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A three inch snowfall was reported at Roan Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours on March 31. A few of the storms became supercells and produced 2 weak tornadoes along with numerous reports of large hail and wind damage. Flash flooding also occurred in parts of Lawrence and Giles Counties where thunderstorms repeatedly moved across the same areas throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding affected southern Giles County. Several roads were impassable and closed due to flooding, including Interstate 65 northbound at mile marker 8 north of Elkton, Highway 31 in Elkton, and Glenn Haven Lane and Jackson Hollow Road in Minor Hill. One person was rescued by emergency personnel from a stranded vehicle on Highway 31 in Elkton, and two other high water rescues were performed elsewhere in the county. Giles County schools were closed on April 1 due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the Southern Plains toward the Mid-Mississippi Valley while a cold front pushed east toward the Mid-South on February 2, 2016. The airmass across the Mid-South was moist and unstable ahead of the front. A couple rounds of showers and thunderstorms occurred. The first round produced heavy rain and some flash flooding in the Memphis Metropolitan area around noon. Another round of showers and thunderstorms developed along the cold front as it moved through the region during the late afternoon. A weak tornado occurred in Crockett County as this round moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in the eastern part of Memphis. The left lane of I-240 at the I-40 interchange was covered with a foot of water. The ramp to I-40 eastbound from Sycamore View Road was also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on February 2, becoming more widespread into the evening before ending early on February 3. A few storms were strong to marginally severe, with some reports of damaging winds received. In addition, thunderstorms repeatedly trained over portions of Hickman, Dickson, and Cheatham Counties, resulting in over 4 inches of rain falling in a few hours and causing major flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across the Natchez Trace Parkway near Highway 64 blocking the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system moved toward the Southern Appalachian Region generating very strong wind gusts in the higher terrain along the Tennessee and North Carolina border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 mph gust was measured at the UT field station near Greenbriar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding affected areas of southern Dickson County from south of I-40 northeastward to near Burns. Several secondary roadways were flooded by up to 1-2 feet of water including Hogan Road, East Piney Road, Gum Branch Road, Stewart Street, Turkey Creek Road, and Highway 46 north of I-40. Several water rescues were conducted including 4 people rescued from a house on Gum Branch Road and 5 people rescued from a house on Turkey Creek Road. The same home on Turkey Creek Road was flooded with at least 1 foot of water, and four cars in the garage of the home were destroyed. One car in the driveway was washed 500 yards down Turkey Creek. At least one dozen homes were impacted by flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another storm system in a very active week of winter weather brought a wintry mix of light snow, light sleet, and light freezing rain to northern Middle Tennessee, generally along and north of the I-40 corridor. Less than 1 inch of combined sleet, ice, and snow fell from Montgomery County eastward to Smith County, while areas east of there in the Upper Cumberland and northern Cumberland Plateau received 2 to 4 inches of snow. The wintry mix caused numerous road accidents and resulted in the closures of I-24 near Clarksville and I-65 north of White House.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snow amounts of 2 to 4 inches were measured across Pickett County. CoCoRaHS station Byrdstown 2.8 SSW measured 3.9 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another storm system in a very active week of winter weather brought a wintry mix of light snow, light sleet, and light freezing rain to northern Middle Tennessee, generally along and north of the I-40 corridor. Less than 1 inch of combined sleet, ice, and snow fell from Montgomery County eastward to Smith County, while areas east of there in the Upper Cumberland and northern Cumberland Plateau received 2 to 4 inches of snow. The wintry mix caused numerous road accidents and resulted in the closures of I-24 near Clarksville and I-65 north of White House.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain fell across Robertson County. The Springfield Fire Department reported 1/4 inch of sleet and ice accumulated in Springfield, with cars encased in ice. The mix of ice, sleet, and snow caused numerous multi-vehicle wrecks along I-65 from White House north to the Kentucky border, resulting in closure of the interstate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front and upper level disturbance produced bands of snow showers during the evening of the 8th through the morning of the 10th.  Snowfall amount of 1-2 inches fell in valley locations, while 4.0 inches fell atop the Cumberland Plateau in Sewanee at the Universiry of the South NWS COOP station.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain fell across Cumberland County. Around 1/4 inch of freezing rain accumulated in far eastern Cumberland County along the east side of the Cumberland Plateau, where cold air was trapped up against the higher terrain. Emergency management and social media reported indicated the ice accumulation in the Ozone, Pineview, and Westel areas knocked down several trees and power lines, with a few road closures and 321 reported power outages. Cumberland County Schools were closed the following day due to the resulting ice damage and power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours on March 31. A few of the storms became supercells and produced 2 weak tornadoes along with numerous reports of large hail and wind damage. Flash flooding also occurred in parts of Lawrence and Giles Counties where thunderstorms repeatedly moved across the same areas throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some trees were blown down across central portions of Dickson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system moved toward the Southern Appalachian Region generating very strong wind gusts in the higher terrain along the Tennessee and North Carolina border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 76 mph gust was measured at the Camp Creek wind tower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough with associated moderate instability moved across the Southern Appalachian Region generating thunderstorms that dropped mostly small hail in a few spots across Tennessee and Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch diameter hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through West Tennessee during the evening hours on March 15, 2016. The front interacted with a marginally moist and unstable airmass resulting in isolated thunderstorms. A couple of storms produced large hail and damaging winds in Tipton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large amount of hail fell one mile south of the intersection of Highway 14 and Highway 59 between Covington and Mason. The hail began as quarter size and diminished to pea size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across West Tennessee during the late afternoon on March 27, 2016. The front interacted with an increasingly moist and unstable airmass to produce scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. A few of these storm became sever with large hail. A tornado touched down in Benton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell north of Milan.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down several miles south of Camden in Benton County. The tornado track was intermittent, with two primary areas of damage. The first area was south of the Old Natchez Trace Trail, from Douglas Drive to Douglas Lane. Numerous trees were uprooted, with the tree fall pattern suggesting a circulation. The second area of damage was between Highway 641 and Old Highway 69. The tornado damaged the roof of a barn. Nearby, the tornado blew in the large door of a metal workshop and removed most of the workshop's door. Portions of the roof landed across old Highway 69. The estimated peak wind was 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours on March 31. A few of the storms became supercells and produced 2 weak tornadoes along with numerous reports of large hail and wind damage. Flash flooding also occurred in parts of Lawrence and Giles Counties where thunderstorms repeatedly moved across the same areas throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down about 4 miles northwest of Hohenwald in northwestern Lewis County. It was a rare, anticyclonic tornado (rotating clockwise). A few trees were uprooted and snapped along Darbytown Road with several large branches downed as well. Minor roof damage occurred to a barn and house along its 1 mile path. At its peak, winds were estimated at 80 mph and the path width was approximately 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours on March 31. A few of the storms became supercells and produced 2 weak tornadoes along with numerous reports of large hail and wind damage. Flash flooding also occurred in parts of Lawrence and Giles Counties where thunderstorms repeatedly moved across the same areas throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down about 7 miles southeast of Linden in eastern Perry County. It weakened to an EF0 in far western Lewis County before lifting. The tornado touched down in Boneyard Hollow where several hundred trees were uprooted, snapped, or downed along its path before entering Lewis County. The tornado weakened to an EF0 there and damaged an outbuilding before lifting 7 miles west of Hohenwald. At its peak, the tornado path width was 350 yards and winds were estimated at 90 mph. The tornado path length was approximately 4 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a departing mesoscale convective system the airmass quickly destabilized across the Mid-South by the late morning hours on March 31, 2016. Meanwhile another potent upper level disturbance moved through the region. As a result numerous showers and thunderstorms erupted across the Mid-South in advance of an approaching cold front.  Numerous severe storms occurred with damaging winds and large hail. A weak tornado also occurred in Hardin County. Storms eventually pushed east as the cold front moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell north of Halls.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees in Drummonds and power poles in Atoka.

A few small trees were also knocked down near the marina. Winds were estimated up to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours on March 31. Flash flooding also occurred in parts of Lawrence and Giles Counties where thunderstorms repeatedly moved across the same areas throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down about 7 miles southeast of Linden in eastern Perry County. It weakened to an EF0 in far western Lewis County before lifting. The tornado touched down in Boneyard Hollow where several hundred trees were uprooted, snapped, or downed along its path before entering Lewis County. The tornado weakened to an EF0 there and damaged an outbuilding before lifting 7 miles west of Hohenwald. At its peak, the tornado path width was 350 yards and winds were estimated at 90 mph. The tornado path length was approximately 4 miles.||This was the first tornado on record to affect Perry County in the month of March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper low pressure system tracked from Eastern Oklahoma toward the Mid-South during the afternoon on March 13, 2016. A moist and unstable airmass was in place across much of Arkansas. Numerous thunderstorms developed and intensified during the afternoon as the upper low pressure system approached. Heavy rain fell over parts of Memphis causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding near Downtown Memphis. One foot of water covered Kansas Street under the Interstate 55 bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through West Tennessee during the evening hours on March 15, 2016. The front interacted with a marginally moist and unstable airmass resulting in isolated thunderstorms. A couple of storms produced large hail and damaging winds in Tipton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail fell in the vicinity of Fort Pillow State Park and Fulton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across West Tennessee during the late afternoon on March 27, 2016. A tornado touched down in Benton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail fell on Stafford Store Road in the southern part of Weakley County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms developed later in the afternoon across Central East Tennessee in a moderately unstable environment across the region. The convective mode was limited to straight line wind damage from discrete cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down across parts of Northern Knox county; including downtown Knoxville, Carter, and Kodak.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail was reported in the downtown area of Sevierville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic heavy rain pattern set up over the Mid-South during the period of March 9th, 2016 to March 13th, 2016. A cold front stretched from Nebraska to Texas on the morning of March 8th. The front moved southeastward and stalled across Southeast Missouri as an upper level low remained stationary over Texas. A very moist atmosphere was in place over the Mid-South south of this boundary. Several weak low pressure centers developed and moved along the stalled front. As a result, periods of heavy rain, up to an inch per hour in some cases, fell over the same locations. Over the several day stretch, the front fluctuated at times shifting the heaviest band of rain. The storm total rainfall over the 5 day period ranged from four to twelve inches. The heavy rain caused widespread flash flooding and many roads were closed as a result. In addition, numerous streams and small rivers overflowed their banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding across all of Shelby County. Several roads and streets were closed as a result including Sam Cooper Boulevard in Midtown and Collierville-Arlington in Collierville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding that closed many roads around Union City including Town and Country Road. A driver was rescued from a car on Knox-Daniel Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of summer heating and high low level moisture content provided the necessary conditions to create a highly unstable environment across the Southern Appalachian region. The instability and more than adequate upper level west to northwesterly flow allowed an mesoscale convective system to remain quite strong as it moved east across the area. Downburst winds in some areas were in the 60 to 70 mile an hour range with speeds as high as 80 miles an hour estimated. Trees were downed across a broad area and resulted in the deaths of three people.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down in Surgoinsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic heavy rain pattern set up over the Mid-South during the period of March 9th, 2016 to March 13th, 2016. A cold front stretched from Nebraska to Texas on the morning of March 8th. The front moved southeastward and stalled across Southeast Missouri as an upper level low remained stationary over Texas. A very moist atmosphere was in place over the Mid-South south of this boundary. Several weak low pressure centers developed and moved along the stalled front. As a result, periods of heavy rain, up to an inch per hour in some cases, fell over the same locations. Over the several day stretch, the front fluctuated at times shifting the heaviest band of rain. The storm total rainfall over the 5 day period ranged from four to twelve inches. The heavy rain caused widespread flash flooding and many roads were closed as a result. In addition, numerous streams and small rivers overflowed their banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding across Carroll County. Mt. Zion Church Road was flooded just east of Huntingdon. About a dozen other county roads were also flooded. Highways 219, 22, 436 and 77 were flooded as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of summer heating and high low level moisture content provided the necessary conditions to create a highly unstable environment across the Southern Appalachian region. The instability and more than adequate upper level west to northwesterly flow allowed an mesoscale convective system to remain quite strong as it moved east across the area. Downburst winds in some areas were in the 60 to 70 mile an hour range with speeds as high as 80 miles an hour estimated. Trees were downed across a broad area and resulted in the deaths of three people.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down in the Oldfort community.

Downburst winds in some areas were in the 60 to 70 mile an hour range with speeds as high as 80 miles an hour estimated. Trees were downed across a broad area and resulted in the deaths of three people.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down in the Whitwell area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small but intense line of thunderstorms moved southwest to northeast across Middle Tennessee during the late afternoon hours on April 6. Numerous reports of wind damage were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in the Pasquo and Temple Hills area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest severe weather event of Spring 2016 across Middle Tennessee affected areas mainly north of I-40 from the late evening hours on May 10 into the morning hours on May 11. Widely scattered supercell thunderstorms moved southeast out of Kentucky, producing dozens of reports of wind damage and large hail up to tennis ball size. Storms began training along an outflow boundary situated northwest to southeast from Robertson County to Cumberland County during the early morning hours on May 11, with heavy rainfall of 3 to 7 inches occurring across Robertson, Sumner, Macon, Trousdale, Wilson, Smith, and Putnam Counties. Major flash flooding across these counties resulted in dozens of homes and businesses flooded, numerous roads being flooded and closed or washed out, and several water rescues from flooded homes and vehicles being conducted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down near Highway 41A.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey determined a small but intense microburst struck the area around Old Lebanon Dirt Road and Chandler Road in western Mount Juliet. Dozens of trees were snapped or uprooted in a roughly 1 mile wide by 1 mile long area with a strong divergent pattern to the east and southeast. A few homes and a church suffered minor roof damage from trees falling onto them and shingles being blown off. Radar measured winds up to 75 mph, and damage was also consistent with a peak wind around 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large but weakening line of showers and thunderstorms moved into Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on May 25. A few reports of minor flooding were received in Montgomery County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported in the Woodlawn and northwest Clarksville areas. Low lying roads near Woodlawn had minor flooding, as well as Peachers Mill Road in Clarksville. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of slow moving showers and thunderstorms moved southward out of Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours on May 26. A few reports of wind damage and flash flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down across Houston County, with one tree down onto a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of slow moving showers and thunderstorms moved southward out of Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours on May 26. A few reports of wind damage and flash flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tSpotter Twitter report and photos indicated a Shell gas station in White Bluff suffered significant damage to the awning covering the gas pumps.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of slow moving showers and thunderstorms moved southward out of Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours on May 26. A few reports of wind damage and flash flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding affected portions of western Sumner County, especially the Millersville and Portland areas. At least one road was flooded in Millersville. Numerous roads were flooded in Portland, including McGlothlin Street and Cody Street, and one water rescue was conducted from a stalled vehicle. No significant damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of slow moving showers and thunderstorms moved southward out of Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours on May 26. A few reports of wind damage and flash flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding affected several parts of Robertson County. The Ridgetop Park area and Lake Road in Greenbrier were flooded, as well as Hoods Branch and the Oak Pointe Subdivision in Coopertown. Several roads and low lying areas of Springfield were also flooded, including Sandy Springs Road, Jarrett Drive, New Chapel Road, Memorial Blvd. and Central Avenue. Parts of Highway 49 East and Highway 52 in Orlinda were flooded and impassable, and the Orlinda Volunteer Fire Station had 8 inches of water inside the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee from the late morning hours into the evening on May 12. A few storms became severe with several reports of wind damage received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tSpotter Twitter report indicated at tree was blown down at Vantage Way and French Landing Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest severe weather event of Spring 2016 across Middle Tennessee affected areas mainly north of I-40 from the late evening hours on May 10 into the morning hours on May 11. Widely scattered supercell thunderstorms moved southeast out of Kentucky, producing dozens of reports of wind damage and large hail up to tennis ball size. Storms began training along an outflow boundary situated northwest to southeast from Robertson County to Cumberland County during the early morning hours on May 11, with heavy rainfall of 3 to 7 inches occurring across Robertson, Sumner, Macon, Trousdale, Wilson, Smith, and Putnam Counties. Major flash flooding across these counties resulted in dozens of homes and businesses flooded, numerous roads being flooded and closed or washed out, and several water rescues from flooded homes and vehicles being conducted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flash flooding affected northern and central Sumner County, especially the Portland, Oak Grove, and Bethpage areas. Numerous primary and secondary roads were flooded and closed or even washed out, including Highway 52, Highway 259, William Mack Lane, Harper Road, North Corinth Road, Cora Street, Harper Road, Jim Courtney Road, and Airport Road in Portland; Duffer Hollow Road, Rock Bridge Road, Chipman Road, Red Tuttle Road, Rogana Road, Alexander Road, and Rock Springs Road in Bethpage; and Highway 25 at the Bledsoe Creek Bridge in Castalian Springs. Several homes and businesses were flooded, including several homes in the Richland subdivision on William Mack Lane in Portland. At least 12 water rescues from vehicles and homes were conducted across Sumner County, and one 90 year old man that was rescued was reportedly transported to the Portland ER Hospital with hypothermia. The flooding across Sumner County was reportedly the worst since the historic May 2010 floods.

Numerous primary and secondary roads along with private driveways and bridges were flooded and closed or even washed out, including Highway 25, Bowmans Branch Lane, Wilburn Hollow Road, and Young Branch Road. One car reportedly was washed away into a creek off Highway 25 in Riddleton. Several homes were also flooded in these same areas. The Smith County Highway Superintendent stated that it would take at least 8 weeks to repair all the flood damage to the road network across the county. At least 2 water rescues from homes were conducted on Wilburn Hollow Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through the Mid-South during the morning hours of April 6, 2016. After the front moved through, gusty southerly winds developed across much of the region. Wind gusts up to 45 mph were reported. Later in the afternoon a cold front moved through and interacted with a marginally unstable airmass to produce numerous showers and scattered thunderstorms. Isolated wind damage occurred in Northwest Tennessee and a couple of lightning related injuries occurred in the Memphis area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two airport workers at Memphis International Airport suffered injuries from a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest severe weather event of Spring 2016 across Middle Tennessee affected areas mainly north of I-40 from the late evening hours on May 10 into the morning hours on May 11. Widely scattered supercell thunderstorms moved southeast out of Kentucky, producing dozens of reports of wind damage and large hail up to tennis ball size. Storms began training along an outflow boundary situated northwest to southeast from Robertson County to Cumberland County during the early morning hours on May 11, with heavy rainfall of 3 to 7 inches occurring across Robertson, Sumner, Macon, Trousdale, Wilson, Smith, and Putnam Counties. Major flash flooding across these counties resulted in dozens of homes and businesses flooded, numerous roads being flooded and closed or washed out, and several water rescues from flooded homes and vehicles being conducted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golfball size hail covered the ground in Pleasant View.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in middle Tennessee and dropped south-southeast into southern middle Tennessee during the late afternoon and early evening hours.  The gust fronts from these storms produced widespread winds of 35-50 mph.  A few more isolated locations likely received winds close to 60 mph.  Numerous reports of trees and tree limbs being knocked down were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of summer heating and high low level moisture content provided the necessary conditions to create a highly unstable environment across the Southern Appalachian region. The instability and more than adequate upper level west to northwesterly flow allowed an mesoscale convective system to remain quite strong as it moved east across the area. Downburst winds in some areas were in the 60 to 70 mile an hour range with speeds as high as 80 miles an hour estimated. Trees were downed across a broad area and resulted in the deaths of three people.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down in Whitwell and vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved southeast from the Upper Midwest/Western Great lakes into the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys by the afternoon of May 1st. A moderately unstable airmass in place contributed to the development of scattered thunderstorms, of which a few became severe east of the Mississippi River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were down from near Higginbotham Road west of Highway 45 to points just southeast of town east of Highway 45 and south of Highway 64.  The hardest hit area was the Selmer Country Club where it appeared a gustnado circulation was seen embedded in the microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remaining moisture and a weak upper level disturbance over the region allowed for a rogue strong storm to form across northern Shelby county on the morning of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell along Highway 385 just east of Highway 204.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection along a slow moving cold front continued to track across the Midsouth, with the most intense storms forming in the mid and late afternoon hours of May 25th. A few storms were capable of severe hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding in Southeast Memphis to include Poplar Avenue and Riverdale Road.  Waist deep water was also reported at a Hickory Hill apartment parking lot.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads closed at Highway 57 and Bethlehem Road, McKinstry Road and Burch Drive, and at the 3800 block of Boothe Road due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moved through the Eastern United States late Tuesday into early Wednesday. The associated surface low pressure system pressed north northeast from the Lower Mississippi across the Mid South on its way across the Lower Ohio Valley. While there were reports of damaging straight line wind with some of the thunderstorms generated ahead of the system cold front, the main and concentrated areas of damage were associated with tornadic supercell thunderstorms. The supercell storms developed in an area of strong shear and sufficient; albeit weak instability. Tornadoes formed in a zone favorable to rotating thunderstorm updrafts just north of a warm frontal boundary across the Tennessee Valley of Alabama northeast through Southeast Tennessee. Three tornadoes developed during the early morning hours on Wednesday, November 30th; impacting McMinn, Polk, Marion, and Sequatchie counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado developed near the intersection of county road 700 and 705 where some minor tree damage was observed. The tornado maintained its intensity as it paralleled Cedar Springs Road and damaged a covered walkway just to the south of Athens along South Jackson Street. It continued to the northeast with the most catastrophic damage along its path occurring along Old Athens Madisonville Road where a few mobile homes were destroyed. The tornado weakened as it moved to the northeast with mostly tree damage; eventually lifting around the intersection of TN-307 and County Road 350. Two people were killed and twenty were injured as the tornado moved across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite widespread and major flooding that affected northern Middle Tennessee during July 2016, a lack of significant rainfall across southern portions of Middle Tennessee from June and continuing through July led to severe (D2) drought conditions into August 2016 across some counties close to the Alabama border. Effects of the drought were generally minor and limited to agricultural interests. Drought conditions improved through the month as more significant rainfall spread across the southern counties of Middle Tennessee, and by the end of August 2016 no counties in the NWS Nashville county warning area were in the severe (D2) drought category per the U.S. Drought Monitor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant lack of rainfall across Middle Tennessee during October 2016, with most areas seeing a rainfall deficit of 2 to 4 inches for the month, led to worsening drought conditions throughout the region. Severe (D2) drought conditions, which were only in place in Grundy County at the beginning of October 2016, spread northwestward into numerous other counties of southern and eastern Middle Tennessee by the end of the month. Extreme (D3) drought conditions even developed across southern portions of Wayne, Lawrence, Giles, Grundy, and Cumberland counties. Effects of these drought conditions led to scattered reports of grass and brush fires in the D3 counties, along with some damage to agriculture.

Several rounds of beneficial rainfall occurred at the end of November, helping to finally alleviate the worst drought conditions since 2007. While improvements are slow, they're steadily continuing to improve with each subsequent round of rainfall across Northern Alabama and Southern Middle Tennessee. The first Drought Monitor (DM) issuance of December finally shows the improvement after no-change was made to the last 2 issuances of the DM in late November. Further improvements are expected heading into the end of the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme Drought (D3) Conditions persist.

Several rounds of beneficial rainfall occurred at the end of November, helping to finally alleviate the worst drought conditions since 2007. While improvements are slow, they're steadily continuing to improve with each subsequent round of rainfall across Northern Alabama and Southern Middle Tennessee. The first Drought Monitor (DM) issuance of December finally shows the improvement after no-change was made to the last 2 issuances of the DM in late November. Further improvements are expected heading into the end of the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought (D4) Conditions persist.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant lack of rainfall across Middle Tennessee during October 2016, with most areas seeing a rainfall deficit of 2 to 4 inches for the month, led to worsening drought conditions throughout the region. Severe (D2) drought conditions, which were only in place in Grundy County at the beginning of October 2016, spread northwestward into numerous other counties of southern and eastern Middle Tennessee by the end of the month. Extreme (D3) drought conditions even developed across southern portions of Wayne, Lawrence, Giles, Grundy, and Cumberland counties. Effects of these drought conditions led to scattered reports of grass and brush fires in the D3 counties, along with some damage to agriculture.

Abnormally dry weather continued during the month of November and fueled the spread of at least moderate (D2) drought conditions over most portions of the Mid-South. A semi-permanent upper ridge of high pressure resulted in little rainfall which continued the drought conditions. Dry conditions negatively impacted pastures, triggering more feeding of hay, and causing concern for hay shortages for the winter months. The drought caused river and lake levels to be at low levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions developed around the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado outbreak occurred during the evening hours of the 29th into the early morning hours of the 30th just to the south in north Alabama. Only one report of thunderstorm wind damage occurred in Lincoln County Tennessee. A very mild and unstable air mass was in place along with a strong southwesterly upper level jet. These ingredients combined with a cold front and pre-frontal trough sweeping from west to east across the area sparked scattered supercell thunderstorms during the evening, a few of which were tornadic. This was followed by a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) that occurred with the trough, which also was responsible for severe weather. Beneficial rainfall occurred with this system with 2-5 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was knocked down onto an abandoned home causing significant damage.  Power lines were also reported down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry weather continued during the month of November and fueled the spread of at least moderate (D2) drought conditions over most portions of the Mid-South. A semi-permanent upper ridge of high pressure resulted in little rainfall which continued the drought conditions. Dry conditions negatively impacted pastures, triggering more feeding of hay, and causing concern for hay shortages for the winter months. The drought caused river and lake levels to be at low levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions developed around the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As cold air rapidly moved into Middle Tennessee behind a strong cold front early on December 18, rain changed to light freezing rain in portions of northern Middle Tennessee. Several reports of a light glaze were received.

Dry conditions negatively impacted pastures, triggering more feeding of hay, and causing concern for hay shortages for the winter months. The drought caused river and lake levels to be at low levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions persisted into December, but ended by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Middle Tennessee during the evening hours on December 17 into the early morning hours on December 18, with a line of strong to severe thunderstorms developing along and ahead of the front. Numerous reports of wind damage were received across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down on Delk Creek Road near Pall Mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Middle Tennessee during the evening hours on December 17 into the early morning hours on December 18, with a line of strong to severe thunderstorms developing along and ahead of the front. Numerous reports of wind damage were received across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey found an intense 1/4 mile wide by 1/2 mile long microburst struck Laguardo. One home was severely damaged by a fallen tree on Northview Circle. Another home lost most of its west facing shingles on Dana Drive which were blown up to 150 yards eastward. A third home was damaged by a fallen tree on Westview Drive. A boat dock on Dana Drive was blown 130 yards to the northeast. Dozens of large trees were snapped or uprooted from Dana Drive and Northview Circle to near Highway 109. Winds were estimated up to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As cold air rapidly moved into Middle Tennessee behind a strong cold front early on December 18, rain changed to light freezing rain in portions of northern Middle Tennessee. Several reports of a light glaze were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of ice was reported on roads and elevated surfaces in Clarksville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread freezing fog occurred across Middle Tennessee during the morning hours on December 20, resulting in icy roads and light snow in some areas. One particular trouble spot was in Giles and Marshall Counties along I-65 where numerous reports of car accidents were received. Further north, the layer of freezing fog was cold enough to produce light snow. Numerous reports of a dusting of snow were received from Summertown, Columbia, and East Nashvill all the way eastward to the Upper Cumberland from Monterey to Jamestown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous car accidents were reported on I-65 and other roads east of I-65 in Giles County at elevations near or above 900 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant rainfall across Middle Tennessee during late November 2016 into December 2016 begin to improve drought conditions throughout the region. Severe (D2) drought conditions across most of Middle Tennessee and extreme (D3) drought conditions in Cumberland County at the beginning of the month improved to moderate (D1) drought or less by the end of the month. Frequent rainfall led to significantly improved conditions for agriculture and area lakes and rivers.

Ongoing drought conditions resulted in very dry fuels across Tennessee. As a result brush fires became more frequent during the month of October. Most were minor, but one in Bartlett damaged vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush fire damaged three pickup trucks at a car dealership in Bartlett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Middle Tennessee during the evening hours on December 17 into the early morning hours on December 18, with a line of strong to severe thunderstorms developing along and ahead of the front. Numerous reports of wind damage were received across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto Benders Ferry Road at Crestview Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant rainfall across Middle Tennessee during late November 2016 into December 2016 begin to improve drought conditions throughout the region. Severe (D2) drought conditions across most of Middle Tennessee and extreme (D3) drought conditions in Cumberland County at the beginning of the month improved to moderate (D1) drought or less by the end of the month. Frequent rainfall led to significantly improved conditions for agriculture and area lakes and rivers.

Only one day after the severe thunderstorms and major flash flooding affected Middle Tennessee on Wednesday July 6 into Thursday July 7, the largest severe weather event of the 2016 summer brought widespread wind damage and some large hail during the afternoon and evening hours on July 8, 2016. During the afternoon, scattered supercell thunderstorms developed across the Upper Cumberland region, with several reports of large hail up to golf ball size and wind damage received. A line of severe thunderstorms then developed across western Kentucky during the late afternoon, then raced southeast across Middle Tennessee through the rest of the evening. These storms produced widespread wind damage to trees and power lines, and knocked out power to nearly 25,000 people in the Nashville metro and up to 50,000 people across Middle Tennessee. Well over 100 reports of wind damage and large hail were received, along with officially measured wind gusts as high as 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued significant lack of rainfall across Middle Tennessee during November 2016 led to worsening drought conditions throughout the region and the driest start to the fall season since 1953. Most of Middle Tennessee received less than one half of an inch of rain through November 22, which is only around 5% of the normal rainfall. As a result, severe (D2) drought conditions spread across all of Middle Tennessee by the middle of the month, with extreme (D3) drought conditions affecting most southern and eastern counties, and even exceptional (D4) drought conditions reaching Cumberland County. Effects of these drought conditions led to many forest, grass and brush fires throughout Middle Tennessee, although no homes, businesses, or other buildings were reportedly burned. Agriculture also continued to suffer losses, and many area lakes and rivers were at unusually low levels for the time of year.||On November 23 and again on November 29-30, separate storm systems brought several inches of significant rainfall to the region, which began to ease drought conditions in many areas.
