A major severe thunderstorm and flash flood event affected Middle Tennessee from Wednesday, July 6 into Thursday, July 7. Three rounds of thunderstorms called Mesoscale Convective Systems (MCS) moved across the area over these two days, with the first MCS occurring on Wednesday afternoon July 6, the second MCS from Wednesday night into Thursday morning July 6-7, and the third MCS during the afternoon of July 7.  The first MCS produced several reports of wind damage across northern parts of Middle Tennessee, while the second MCS produced scattered wind damage and dropped 3 to 8 of rain, which caused major flash flooding over northern Middle Tennessee. The final MCS on Thursday afternoon July 7 brought another round of more widespread wind damage.||Based on reports from the Tennessee Emergency Management Agency and local media, the flash flooding damaged or destroyed over 65 homes, businesses, and other buildings across Stewart, Montgomery, Robertson, Sumner, and Cheatham Counties, as well as washed out numerous roads and bridges. Amazingly, no injuries or fatalities were reported despite the widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve trees were reported blown down across Davidson County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding affected much of Davidson County, with several homes and roads damaged by flooding. Homes that were flooded included several on Brick Church Pike at Ewingdale Drive in north Nashville, where many residents were evacuated due to the flooding. Roads that were flooded and closed included I-24 East at Shelby Avenue, I-40 West near Spence Lane, Church Street at I-40 in downtown Nashville which was blocked by 2 to 3 feet of water, Murfreesboro Pike at Menzler Road, Neelys Bend Road near Gallatin Pike, Old Hickory Blvd near Carney Road, Brick Church Pike at Ewingdale Drive, Charlotte Pike at American Road in west Nashville, and Shute Lane at Saundersville Road in Old Hickory. No injuries or fatalities were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered showers and thunderstorms affected southern Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on July 18. One isolated microburst caused wind damage in Pulaski.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Giles County Emergency Manager reported a microburst struck Pulaski from the industrial area on the northwest side of town into downtown. Photos showed 4 utility poles were snapped on Industrial Blvd and Bennet Drive. Several trees were also blown down, including one tree falling on a car at 5th Street and Flower Street, and another tree down on East Madison Street. Winds were estimated around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on July 23. One isolated severe thunderstorms caused wind damage in Bedford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down with power outages in Shelbyville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on July 22. One isolated severe thunderstorms caused wind damage across parts of west Nashville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tSpotter Twitter, media, and fire department reports indicated a roughly 2 mile wide downburst struck the Belle Meade, Charlotte Park and The Nations areas of west Nashville in a southeast to northwest direction. Several trees and tree branches were blown down, including a snapped pine tree at the Rock Harbor Condos on Basswood Avenue, a tree down on a house on Charlotte Pike, and a tree down on Brook Hollow Road. Many power lines were also blown down with widespread power outages. A large scaffolding structure for a water tower construction project on Morrow Road in The Nations collapsed. This was the second time the scaffolding collapsed due to severe thunderstorm winds, with the first time being on June 15, 2016. Pea size hail was also reported in the area. Winds were estimated around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on July 3. A few storms became strong to severe in White and Cumberland Counties, with some reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down on Webbs Camp Road near Walling.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down on Allen Drive and another tree was blown down on Highway 70 South in Sparta.

A continued significant lack of rainfall across Middle Tennessee during November 2016 led to worsening drought conditions throughout the region and the driest start to the fall season since 1953. Most of Middle Tennessee received less than one half of an inch of rain through November 22, which is only around 5% of the normal rainfall. As a result, severe (D2) drought conditions spread across all of Middle Tennessee by the middle of the month, with extreme (D3) drought conditions affecting most southern and eastern counties, and even exceptional (D4) drought conditions reaching Cumberland County. Effects of these drought conditions led to many forest, grass and brush fires throughout Middle Tennessee, although no homes, businesses, or other buildings were reportedly burned. Agriculture also continued to suffer losses, and many area lakes and rivers were at unusually low levels for the time of year.||On November 23 and again on November 29-30, separate storm systems brought several inches of significant rainfall to the region, which began to ease drought conditions in many areas.

A major severe thunderstorm and flash flood event affected Middle Tennessee from Wednesday, July 6 into Thursday, July 7. Three rounds of thunderstorms called Mesoscale Convective Systems (MCS) moved across the area over these two days, with the first MCS occurring on Wednesday afternoon July 6, the second MCS from Wednesday night into Thursday morning July 6-7, and the third MCS during the afternoon of July 7.  The first MCS produced several reports of wind damage across northern parts of Middle Tennessee, while the second MCS produced scattered wind damage and dropped 3 to 8 of rain, which caused major flash flooding over northern Middle Tennessee. The final MCS on Thursday afternoon July 7 brought another round of more widespread wind damage.||Based on reports from the Tennessee Emergency Management Agency and local media, the flash flooding damaged or destroyed over 65 homes, businesses, and other buildings across Stewart, Montgomery, Robertson, Sumner, and Cheatham Counties, as well as washed out numerous roads and bridges. Amazingly, no injuries or fatalities were reported despite the widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Castalian Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Only one day after the severe thunderstorms and major flash flooding affected Middle Tennessee on Wednesday July 6 into Thursday July 7, the largest severe weather event of the 2016 summer brought widespread wind damage and some large hail during the afternoon and evening hours on July 8, 2016. During the afternoon, scattered supercell thunderstorms developed across the Upper Cumberland region, with several reports of large hail up to golf ball size and wind damage received. A line of severe thunderstorms then developed across western Kentucky during the late afternoon, then raced southeast across Middle Tennessee through the rest of the evening. These storms produced widespread wind damage to trees and power lines, and knocked out power to nearly 25,000 people in the Nashville metro and up to 50,000 people across Middle Tennessee. Well over 100 reports of wind damage and large hail were received, along with officially measured wind gusts as high as 61 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Facebook photo showed a large tree blown down that blocked Highway 85 in Alpine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on July 19 across Middle Tennessee. Several reports of wind damage and large hail were received, including a very damaging microburst in Williamson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports from emergency management, broadcast media and social media indicated a small but intense 1.5 mile long by 1 mile wide microburst struck the Brentmeade subdivision of Brentwood. Numerous trees 1 to 2 feet in diameter were snapped or uprooted in a general westerly direction from 9215 Old Smyrna Road west-southwestward to Wilson Pike, including on Shenandoah Drive, Oden Court, Davis Drive, Hood Place, and Saddlebow Drive. One large tree fell onto a home on Oden Court at Wilson Pike, and another tree fell onto a home on Brushboro Drive. Two power poles were snapped and several power lines were blown down, including at 9121 Oden Court, 9217 Foxland Drive, 822 Coxboro Drive, 717 Shenadoah Drive, and on Davis Drive. Power was knocked out to 8350 customers. Winds were estimated up to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major severe thunderstorm and flash flood event affected Middle Tennessee from Wednesday, July 6 into Thursday, July 7. Three rounds of thunderstorms called Mesoscale Convective Systems (MCS) moved across the area over these two days, with the first MCS occurring on Wednesday afternoon July 6, the second MCS from Wednesday night into Thursday morning July 6-7, and the third MCS during the afternoon of July 7.  The first MCS produced several reports of wind damage across northern parts of Middle Tennessee, while the second MCS produced scattered wind damage and dropped 3 to 8 of rain, which caused major flash flooding over northern Middle Tennessee. The final MCS on Thursday afternoon July 7 brought another round of more widespread wind damage.||Based on reports from the Tennessee Emergency Management Agency and local media, the flash flooding damaged or destroyed over 65 homes, businesses, and other buildings across Stewart, Montgomery, Robertson, Sumner, and Cheatham Counties, as well as washed out numerous roads and bridges. Amazingly, no injuries or fatalities were reported despite the widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Historic flash flooding affected much of Stewart County, especially areas northeast of Dover. Dozens of homes, businesses, vehicles and roads were damaged or destroyed by flooding across Stewart County, mainly in the Big Rock and Bumpus Mills communities which were devastated by the flooding. Several water rescues were also conducted for people trapped in flooded homes and vehicles. TEMA reported 10 homes were destroyed, 7 homes had major damage, 10 mobile homes were destroyed, Big Rock Baptist Church was destroyed, Big Rock Church of Christ and Dyers Creek Church of God had major damage, 1 park had major damage, The Homestead Child Day Care Center on Robertson Hill Road was destroyed, and a Subway restaurant on Highway 79 at Joiner Hollow Road had major damage. Some of the roads that were flooded and closed included Highway 120 in Bumpus Mills, and Joiner Hollow Road, Earl Coppage Road, Mildred George Road, and Big Rock Road in Big Rock which were all washed out. No injuries or fatalities were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued significant lack of rainfall across Middle Tennessee during November 2016 led to worsening drought conditions throughout the region and the driest start to the fall season since 1953. Most of Middle Tennessee received less than one half of an inch of rain through November 22, which is only around 5% of the normal rainfall. As a result, severe (D2) drought conditions spread across all of Middle Tennessee by the middle of the month, with extreme (D3) drought conditions affecting most southern and eastern counties, and even exceptional (D4) drought conditions reaching Cumberland County. Effects of these drought conditions led to many forest, grass and brush fires throughout Middle Tennessee, although no homes, businesses, or other buildings were reportedly burned. Agriculture also continued to suffer losses, and many area lakes and rivers were at unusually low levels for the time of year.||On November 23 and again on November 29-30, separate storm systems brought several inches of significant rainfall to the region, which began to ease drought conditions in many areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flash flooding affected much of Sumner County, especially in and near Cottontown, Bethpage, Hendersonville, Gallatin, and Castalian Springs, with numerous homes, businesses, vehicles and roads damaged by flooding. TEMA reported 30 homes were damaged and 4 bridges severely damaged by the flash flooding. Homes were flooded in locations such as Tyree Springs Road in Hendersonville, where residents were trapped by flood waters; Stoneridge Farms Apartments at 2325 Nashville Pike in Gallatin; and on Old Douglas Road in Gallatin, where one home was inundated by three feet of water. Businesses that were flooded included Long Hollow Nursery around 6 miles west of Gallatin. Roads that were flooded and closed in Hendersonville included Lower Station Camp Creek Road between Saundersville Road and Jenkins Lane, Indian Lake Road and Drakes Creek Road at Stop Thirty Road. In Cottontown, roads that were impassable due to flood waters included Bugg Hollow Road where Briggance Branch and Station Camp Creek flooded the roadway, and Pee Dee Branch Road at Upper Station Camp Creek Road. Other flooded roadways were Old Douglas Road in Gallatin, Old Hopewell Road near Lauderdale Lane in Bethpage which was washed out, Deshea Creek Road northeast of Gallatin which was washed out, and Saddle Club Road in Westmoreland which was also washed out. One man had to be rescued from clinging to a tree after his vehicle was washed away in flood waters on Liberty Lane about 5 miles northwest of Gallatin. Another man was rescued after driving into flood waters in Castalian Springs. No injuries or fatalities were reported.

The final MCS on Thursday afternoon July 7 brought another round of more widespread wind damage.||Based on reports from the Tennessee Emergency Management Agency and local media, the flash flooding damaged or destroyed over 65 homes, businesses, and other buildings across Stewart, Montgomery, Robertson, Sumner, and Cheatham Counties, as well as washed out numerous roads and bridges. Amazingly, no injuries or fatalities were reported despite the widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down on Spring Street at Dawson Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Only one day after the severe thunderstorms and major flash flooding affected Middle Tennessee on Wednesday July 6 into Thursday July 7, the largest severe weather event of the 2016 summer brought widespread wind damage and some large hail during the afternoon and evening hours on July 8, 2016. During the afternoon, scattered supercell thunderstorms developed across the Upper Cumberland region, with several reports of large hail up to golf ball size and wind damage received. A line of severe thunderstorms then developed across western Kentucky during the late afternoon, then raced southeast across Middle Tennessee through the rest of the evening. These storms produced widespread wind damage to trees and power lines, and knocked out power to nearly 25,000 people in the Nashville metro and up to 50,000 people across Middle Tennessee. Well over 100 reports of wind damage and large hail were received, along with officially measured wind gusts as high as 61 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down with power outages in the Spring Creek subdivision on Eastridge Road in Woodlawn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A localized but significant flash flood event affected portions of northern Middle Tennessee during the late morning and afternoon hours on July 29. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the morning hours then moved eastward, with some showers and storms continuing to train over the same areas into the afternoon. The resulting heavy rainfall led to flash flooding across portions of eastern Sumner County, southwest Macon County, Trousdale County, northern Smith County, and northern Wilson County, where radar estimated 2 to 4 inch rainfall totals. Several homes, buildings, vehicles, and roads were flooded in these areas, with the worst flooding across eastern Sumner County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding affected eastern Sumner County, especially the Bransford, Westmoreland and Bethpage areas along the Bledsoe Creek watershed, with several homes, buildings, vehicles and roads damaged by flooding. Roads that were flooded and closed included Phillips Hollow Road, Highway 31E at Deshea Creek, Haze Hyde Hollow Road, Pumping Station Road, Wolf Hill Road, and Old Wolf Hill Road. Four water rescues were conducted, including four people who were rescued from a flooded home at 125 Phillips Hollow Road. An unoccupied car at the home was also swept away in the flood waters. Several homes, farm buildings, and a church were flooded or surrounded by flood waters along Highway 31E near Wolf Hill Road in the Bransford community, as well as along Pumping Station Road in Westmoreland. No injuries or fatalities were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding affected southwestern Macon County, including areas around Lafayette and Siloam, with several roads flooded or damaged by flood waters. Roads that were flooded and closed included Highway 141, Epperson Springs Road, Brattontown Circle, Phillips Hollow Road, Leaths Branch Road, Hicks Hollow Road, and Green Grove Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued significant lack of rainfall across Middle Tennessee during November 2016 led to worsening drought conditions throughout the region and the driest start to the fall season since 1953. Most of Middle Tennessee received less than one half of an inch of rain through November 22, which is only around 5% of the normal rainfall. As a result, severe (D2) drought conditions spread across all of Middle Tennessee by the middle of the month, with extreme (D3) drought conditions affecting most southern and eastern counties, and even exceptional (D4) drought conditions reaching Cumberland County. Effects of these drought conditions led to many forest, grass and brush fires throughout Middle Tennessee, although no homes, businesses, or other buildings were reportedly burned. Agriculture also continued to suffer losses, and many area lakes and rivers were at unusually low levels for the time of year.||On November 23 and again on November 29-30, separate storm systems brought several inches of significant rainfall to the region, which began to ease drought conditions in many areas.

A line of severe thunderstorms then developed across western Kentucky during the late afternoon, then raced southeast across Middle Tennessee through the rest of the evening. These storms produced widespread wind damage to trees and power lines, and knocked out power to nearly 25,000 people in the Nashville metro and up to 50,000 people across Middle Tennessee. Well over 100 reports of wind damage and large hail were received, along with officially measured wind gusts as high as 61 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in the 1000 block of Statesville Road in Watertown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure caused numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across northwest Middle Tennessee late on July 27 then slowly spread eastward across Middle Tennessee on July 28. Several reports of heavy rainfall and some flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets were flooded and closed in Erin including McMillian Street, Front Street, Maple Street, and Mobley Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure caused numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across northwest Middle Tennessee late on July 27 then slowly spread eastward across Middle Tennessee on July 28. Several reports of heavy rainfall and some flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS station Tennessee Ridge 1.4 SW measured a 24 hour rainfall total of 5.30 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS station Tennessee Ridge 3.0 S measured a 48 hour rainfall total of 6.10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure caused numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across northwest Middle Tennessee late on July 27 then slowly spread eastward across Middle Tennessee on July 28. Several reports of heavy rainfall and some flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS station Clarksville 5.4 WSW measured a 24 hour rainfall total of 4.01 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A localized but significant flash flood event affected portions of northern Middle Tennessee during the late morning and afternoon hours on July 29. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the morning hours then moved eastward, with some showers and storms continuing to train over the same areas into the afternoon. The resulting heavy rainfall led to flash flooding across portions of eastern Sumner County, southwest Macon County, Trousdale County, northern Smith County, and northern Wilson County, where radar estimated 2 to 4 inch rainfall totals. Several homes, buildings, vehicles, and roads were flooded in these areas, with the worst flooding across eastern Sumner County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding affected portions of northern Wilson County, with several roads, buildings, and vehicles flooded.  Roads that were flooded and closed including Highway 231 near Carroll Oakland Elementary School, the 3000 block of Cedar Grove Road, Old Hunters Point Pike, and Old Rome Pike in Bellwood. A garage was also flooded on Old Hunters Point Pike. Two people were rescued from a stranded vehicle on Cedar Grove Road at Old Hunters Point Pike. No injuries or fatalities were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A localized but significant flash flood event affected portions of northern Middle Tennessee during the late morning and afternoon hours on July 29. The resulting heavy rainfall led to flash flooding across portions of eastern Sumner County, southwest Macon County, Trousdale County, northern Smith County, and northern Wilson County, where radar estimated 2 to 4 inch rainfall totals. Several homes, buildings, vehicles, and roads were flooded in these areas, with the worst flooding across eastern Sumner County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding affected portions of northern Smith County per Facebook and tSpotter Twitter reports. Several roads were flooded and closed in Pleasant Shade, while Bowmans Branch Lane in Riddleton was flooded with one bridge partially washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms spread eastward across Middle Tennessee during the morning and early afternoon hours on July 14. Several reports of wind damage and large hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported near Highway 109 just south of I-40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued significant lack of rainfall across Middle Tennessee during November 2016 led to worsening drought conditions throughout the region and the driest start to the fall season since 1953. Most of Middle Tennessee received less than one half of an inch of rain through November 22, which is only around 5% of the normal rainfall. As a result, severe (D2) drought conditions spread across all of Middle Tennessee by the middle of the month, with extreme (D3) drought conditions affecting most southern and eastern counties, and even exceptional (D4) drought conditions reaching Cumberland County. Effects of these drought conditions led to many forest, grass and brush fires throughout Middle Tennessee, although no homes, businesses, or other buildings were reportedly burned. Agriculture also continued to suffer losses, and many area lakes and rivers were at unusually low levels for the time of year.||On November 23 and again on November 29-30, separate storm systems brought several inches of significant rainfall to the region, which began to ease drought conditions in many areas.

A major severe thunderstorm and flash flood event affected Middle Tennessee from Wednesday, July 6 into Thursday, July 7. Three rounds of thunderstorms called Mesoscale Convective Systems (MCS) moved across the area over these two days, with the first MCS occurring on Wednesday afternoon July 6, the second MCS from Wednesday night into Thursday morning July 6-7, and the third MCS during the afternoon of July 7.  The first MCS produced several reports of wind damage across northern parts of Middle Tennessee, while the second MCS produced scattered wind damage and dropped 3 to 8 of rain, which caused major flash flooding over northern Middle Tennessee. The final MCS on Thursday afternoon July 7 brought another round of more widespread wind damage.||Based on reports from the Tennessee Emergency Management Agency and local media, the flash flooding damaged or destroyed over 65 homes, businesses, and other buildings across Stewart, Montgomery, Robertson, Sumner, and Cheatham Counties, as well as washed out numerous roads and bridges. Amazingly, no injuries or fatalities were reported despite the widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on power line across from Dollar General on Highway 79.

Well over 100 reports of wind damage and large hail were received, along with officially measured wind gusts as high as 61 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were blown down on Kingston Road at Highway 96 near Fairview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued significant lack of rainfall across Middle Tennessee during November 2016 led to worsening drought conditions throughout the region and the driest start to the fall season since 1953. Most of Middle Tennessee received less than one half of an inch of rain through November 22, which is only around 5% of the normal rainfall. As a result, severe (D2) drought conditions spread across all of Middle Tennessee by the middle of the month, with extreme (D3) drought conditions affecting most southern and eastern counties, and even exceptional (D4) drought conditions reaching Cumberland County. Effects of these drought conditions led to many forest, grass and brush fires throughout Middle Tennessee, although no homes, businesses, or other buildings were reportedly burned. Agriculture also continued to suffer losses, and many area lakes and rivers were at unusually low levels for the time of year.||On November 23 and again on November 29-30, separate storm systems brought several inches of significant rainfall to the region, which began to ease drought conditions in many areas.

A major severe thunderstorm and flash flood event affected Middle Tennessee from Wednesday, July 6 into Thursday, July 7. Three rounds of thunderstorms called Mesoscale Convective Systems (MCS) moved across the area over these two days, with the first MCS occurring on Wednesday afternoon July 6, the second MCS from Wednesday night into Thursday morning July 6-7, and the third MCS during the afternoon of July 7.  The first MCS produced several reports of wind damage across northern parts of Middle Tennessee, while the second MCS produced scattered wind damage and dropped 3 to 8 of rain, which caused major flash flooding over northern Middle Tennessee. The final MCS on Thursday afternoon July 7 brought another round of more widespread wind damage.||Based on reports from the Tennessee Emergency Management Agency and local media, the flash flooding damaged or destroyed over 65 homes, businesses, and other buildings across Stewart, Montgomery, Robertson, Sumner, and Cheatham Counties, as well as washed out numerous roads and bridges. Amazingly, no injuries or fatalities were reported despite the widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tSpotter Twitter report indicated numerous trees were blown down along a 2 to 3 mile stretch of Hiwasee Road near Cedar Bluff Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough of low pressure combined with a northward moving warm front to spawn numerous supercells and tornadoes across the southeastern US from the afternoon hours on November 29, 2016 through the night and into the next day on November 30. Numerous tornado touched down in Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, Middle and Eastern Tennessee, Georgia, Florida, and South Carolina, resulting in 5 deaths and dozens of injuries. Across Middle Tennessee, two tornadoes touched down in Coffee County in Tullahoma and near Manchester, damaging many homes and causing one minor injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down just inside the Coffee County line west of Tullahoma. This tornado tracked through the northside of Tullahoma crossing over State Route 16/Jackson Street damaging several businesses with mostly roof damage observed. The tornado then moved across the Lakewood Golf and Country Club and Lake Tullahoma Estates neighborhoods where several hundred trees were uprooted and snapped. Almost all of the structural damage in these neighborhoods were due to downed trees. One person sustained a minor injury when a tree fell on their car. Once the tornado crossed the lake, it lifted just before Cherry Springs Road.

Across Middle Tennessee, two tornadoes touched down in Coffee County in Tullahoma and near Manchester, damaging many homes and causing one minor injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A carport was blown off into power lines at Mountain Auto Sales on Highway 41 just east of Monteagle. In addition, several trees and power lines were blown down along Highway 41 and Dubose Street in the same area with both roadways blocked and closed. Grundy County schools were closed 11/30 due to the storm damage between Monteagle and Tracy City.

Three rounds of thunderstorms called Mesoscale Convective Systems (MCS) moved across the area over these two days, with the first MCS occurring on Wednesday afternoon July 6, the second MCS from Wednesday night into Thursday morning July 6-7, and the third MCS during the afternoon of July 7.  The first MCS produced several reports of wind damage across northern parts of Middle Tennessee, while the second MCS produced scattered wind damage and dropped 3 to 8 of rain, which caused major flash flooding over northern Middle Tennessee. The final MCS on Thursday afternoon July 7 brought another round of more widespread wind damage.||Based on reports from the Tennessee Emergency Management Agency and local media, the flash flooding damaged or destroyed over 65 homes, businesses, and other buildings across Stewart, Montgomery, Robertson, Sumner, and Cheatham Counties, as well as washed out numerous roads and bridges. Amazingly, no injuries or fatalities were reported despite the widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding affected several portions of Cheatham County. TEMA reported one home was flooded and one road was flooded and closed. A tSpotter Twitter report and photo also showed Lockertsville Road at Harristown Road was flooded and impassable. No injuries or fatalities were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Only one day after the severe thunderstorms and major flash flooding affected Middle Tennessee on Wednesday July 6 into Thursday July 7, the largest severe weather event of the 2016 summer brought widespread wind damage and some large hail during the afternoon and evening hours on July 8, 2016. During the afternoon, scattered supercell thunderstorms developed across the Upper Cumberland region, with several reports of large hail up to golf ball size and wind damage received. A line of severe thunderstorms then developed across western Kentucky during the late afternoon, then raced southeast across Middle Tennessee through the rest of the evening. These storms produced widespread wind damage to trees and power lines, and knocked out power to nearly 25,000 people in the Nashville metro and up to 50,000 people across Middle Tennessee. Well over 100 reports of wind damage and large hail were received, along with officially measured wind gusts as high as 61 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree and power lines were blown down on North Corinth Road east of Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued significant lack of rainfall across Middle Tennessee during November 2016 led to worsening drought conditions throughout the region and the driest start to the fall season since 1953. Most of Middle Tennessee received less than one half of an inch of rain through November 22, which is only around 5% of the normal rainfall. As a result, severe (D2) drought conditions spread across all of Middle Tennessee by the middle of the month, with extreme (D3) drought conditions affecting most southern and eastern counties, and even exceptional (D4) drought conditions reaching Cumberland County. Effects of these drought conditions led to many forest, grass and brush fires throughout Middle Tennessee, although no homes, businesses, or other buildings were reportedly burned. Agriculture also continued to suffer losses, and many area lakes and rivers were at unusually low levels for the time of year.||On November 23 and again on November 29-30, separate storm systems brought several inches of significant rainfall to the region, which began to ease drought conditions in many areas.

The final MCS on Thursday afternoon July 7 brought another round of more widespread wind damage.||Based on reports from the Tennessee Emergency Management Agency and local media, the flash flooding damaged or destroyed over 65 homes, businesses, and other buildings across Stewart, Montgomery, Robertson, Sumner, and Cheatham Counties, as well as washed out numerous roads and bridges. Amazingly, no injuries or fatalities were reported despite the widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail was reported near Franklin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Only one day after the severe thunderstorms and major flash flooding affected Middle Tennessee on Wednesday July 6 into Thursday July 7, the largest severe weather event of the 2016 summer brought widespread wind damage and some large hail during the afternoon and evening hours on July 8, 2016. During the afternoon, scattered supercell thunderstorms developed across the Upper Cumberland region, with several reports of large hail up to golf ball size and wind damage received. A line of severe thunderstorms then developed across western Kentucky during the late afternoon, then raced southeast across Middle Tennessee through the rest of the evening. These storms produced widespread wind damage to trees and power lines, and knocked out power to nearly 25,000 people in the Nashville metro and up to 50,000 people across Middle Tennessee. Well over 100 reports of wind damage and large hail were received, along with officially measured wind gusts as high as 61 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and crushed a fence on Old Springfield Pike near Goodlettsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 60 knot low level jet developed across the Southern Appalachian Region in advance of an upper level trough driving northeast across the Great Lakes. Strong wind gusts were measured across the higher terrain along the Tennessee and North Carolina border as well as terrain in Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 83 mph wind gust was measured at Cove Mountain Tower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued significant lack of rainfall across Middle Tennessee during November 2016 led to worsening drought conditions throughout the region and the driest start to the fall season since 1953. Most of Middle Tennessee received less than one half of an inch of rain through November 22, which is only around 5% of the normal rainfall. As a result, severe (D2) drought conditions spread across all of Middle Tennessee by the middle of the month, with extreme (D3) drought conditions affecting most southern and eastern counties, and even exceptional (D4) drought conditions reaching Cumberland County. Effects of these drought conditions led to many forest, grass and brush fires throughout Middle Tennessee, although no homes, businesses, or other buildings were reportedly burned. Agriculture also continued to suffer losses, and many area lakes and rivers were at unusually low levels for the time of year.||On November 23 and again on November 29-30, separate storm systems brought several inches of significant rainfall to the region, which began to ease drought conditions in many areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued significant lack of rainfall across Middle Tennessee during November 2016 led to worsening drought conditions throughout the region and the driest start to the fall season since 1953. Most of Middle Tennessee received less than one half of an inch of rain through November 22, which is only around 5% of the normal rainfall. As a result, severe (D2) drought conditions spread across all of Middle Tennessee by the middle of the month, with extreme (D3) drought conditions affecting most southern and eastern counties, and even exceptional (D4) drought conditions reaching Cumberland County. Effects of these drought conditions led to many forest, grass and brush fires throughout Middle Tennessee, although no homes, businesses, or other buildings were reportedly burned. Agriculture also continued to suffer losses, and many area lakes and rivers were at unusually low levels for the time of year.||On November 23 and again on November 29-30, separate storm systems brought several inches of significant rainfall to the region, which began to ease drought conditions in many areas.

A large upper level trough of low pressure combined with a northward moving warm front to spawn numerous supercells and tornadoes across the southeastern US from the afternoon hours on November 29, 2016 through the night and into the next day on November 30. Numerous tornado touched down in Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, Middle and Eastern Tennessee, Georgia, Florida, and South Carolina, resulting in 5 deaths and dozens of injuries. Across Middle Tennessee, two tornadoes touched down in Coffee County in Tullahoma and near Manchester, damaging many homes and causing one minor injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A new circulation formed east-southeast of the Tullahoma tornado and touched down just southeast of Manchester. Although not on the ground for long, several dozen trees were snapped and uprooted and several barns and outbuildings were heavily damaged along the path. A one story log cabin style house was picked up off of the foundation and moved 6-8 feet off of center with most of the house still fully intact. Unfortunately, this house was not properly attached to the foundation hence the EF-1 rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major severe thunderstorm and flash flood event affected Middle Tennessee from Wednesday, July 6 into Thursday, July 7. Three rounds of thunderstorms called Mesoscale Convective Systems (MCS) moved across the area over these two days, with the first MCS occurring on Wednesday afternoon July 6, the second MCS from Wednesday night into Thursday morning July 6-7, and the third MCS during the afternoon of July 7.  The first MCS produced several reports of wind damage across northern parts of Middle Tennessee, while the second MCS produced scattered wind damage and dropped 3 to 8 of rain, which caused major flash flooding over northern Middle Tennessee. The final MCS on Thursday afternoon July 7 brought another round of more widespread wind damage.||Based on reports from the Tennessee Emergency Management Agency and local media, the flash flooding damaged or destroyed over 65 homes, businesses, and other buildings across Stewart, Montgomery, Robertson, Sumner, and Cheatham Counties, as well as washed out numerous roads and bridges. Amazingly, no injuries or fatalities were reported despite the widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reportedly blown down in Cross Plains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flash flooding affected much of Robertson County, with several homes, businesses, and roads damaged by flooding. Homes that were flooded included several on Owens Chapel Road along Sulfer Fork Creek around 4 miles northeast of Springfield, where several people were rescued from flooded homes. Businesses that were flooded in Springfield included a trucking depot on Highway 41 North and a Little Caesars Pizza, where the roof caved in due to the amount of heavy rain on the roof. American Legion Baseball Park at 4660 Highway 41 North and the Springfield Greenway were also submerged by flood waters. Roads that were flooded and impassable included several streets in Springfield such as 11th Avenue East, where some residents trapped in homes, and Armstrong Road around 6 miles east of Springfield. No injuries or fatalities were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Only one day after the severe thunderstorms and major flash flooding affected Middle Tennessee on Wednesday July 6 into Thursday July 7, the largest severe weather event of the 2016 summer brought widespread wind damage and some large hail during the afternoon and evening hours on July 8, 2016. During the afternoon, scattered supercell thunderstorms developed across the Upper Cumberland region, with several reports of large hail up to golf ball size and wind damage received. A line of severe thunderstorms then developed across western Kentucky during the late afternoon, then raced southeast across Middle Tennessee through the rest of the evening. These storms produced widespread wind damage to trees and power lines, and knocked out power to nearly 25,000 people in the Nashville metro and up to 50,000 people across Middle Tennessee. Well over 100 reports of wind damage and large hail were received, along with officially measured wind gusts as high as 61 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radio station WDEB relayed report of a funnel cloud in the Muddy Pond community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued significant lack of rainfall across Middle Tennessee during November 2016 led to worsening drought conditions throughout the region and the driest start to the fall season since 1953. Most of Middle Tennessee received less than one half of an inch of rain through November 22, which is only around 5% of the normal rainfall. As a result, severe (D2) drought conditions spread across all of Middle Tennessee by the middle of the month, with extreme (D3) drought conditions affecting most southern and eastern counties, and even exceptional (D4) drought conditions reaching Cumberland County. Effects of these drought conditions led to many forest, grass and brush fires throughout Middle Tennessee, although no homes, businesses, or other buildings were reportedly burned. Agriculture also continued to suffer losses, and many area lakes and rivers were at unusually low levels for the time of year.||On November 23 and again on November 29-30, separate storm systems brought several inches of significant rainfall to the region, which began to ease drought conditions in many areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flash flooding affected much of Montgomery County, with several homes, businesses, vehicles and roads damaged by flooding. Several water rescues were also conducted for people trapped in flooded homes and vehicles in Clarksville. Homes that were flooded included at least one on Central Avenue in Clarksville, where 2 people were rescued from a flooded home, and others on Golf Club Lane. Businesses damaged by flooding in Clarksville included Swan Lake Golf Course on Dunbar Cave Road and the Tabernacle Church on Golf Club Lane. Roads that were flooded and closed in Clarksville included Liberty Parkway, Crossland Avenue, Central Avenue, Golf Club Lane, Madison Street, University Avenue, Riverside Drive, Highway 41A near Eva Lane, and Saratoga Drive, where one person was rescued from a flooded car. The flooding also caused large sinkholes to open on Spencer Lane at Jackson Road, and Mossrose Road between West Happy Hollow Drive and Woody Hills Drive, forcing the closure of both roads. Flooded and closed roads elsewhere in Montgomery County, mainly western portions of the county, included Cooper Creek Road, Lylewood Road, Lake Road, Moore Hollow Road, and Dotsonville Road, which had a bridge damaged by flooding. Numerous parks were flooded and closed in Clarksville as well, which encompassed Billy Dunlop Park, Clarksville Greenway, Heritage Park, Kleeman Recreation Center, McGregor Park, and the New Providence Pool. No injuries or fatalities were reported.

A strong upper level system moved through the Eastern United States late Tuesday into early Wednesday. The associated surface low pressure system pressed north northeast from the Lower Mississippi across the Mid South on its way across the Lower Ohio Valley. While there were reports of damaging straight line wind with some of the thunderstorms generated ahead of the system cold front, the main and concentrated areas of damage were associated with tornadic supercell thunderstorms. The supercell storms developed in an area of strong shear and sufficient; albeit weak instability. Tornadoes formed in a zone favorable to rotating thunderstorm updrafts just north of a warm frontal boundary across the Tennessee Valley of Alabama northeast through Southeast Tennessee. Three tornadoes developed during the early morning hours on Wednesday, November 30th; impacting McMinn, Polk, Marion, and Sequatchie counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed in far northern Marion County, about one half mile from the Sequatchie County border and quickly intensified to a strong EF-2 tornado with winds of 130 mph as it crossed into Sequatchie County. A double wide mobile home was completely destroyed and an adjacent church roof was heavily damaged. The tornado passed through a grove of trees snapping nearly all of them halfway up the trunk. The strong tornado passed through a farm and caused significant damage to a two story family home. This part of the tornado path was determined to be at the high end of the EF-2 rating. As the tornado continued along a northeasterly track, two additional homes were heavily damaged before moving along an unpopulated ridge. The tornado likely skipped along this ridge as it crossed Highway 127 and then crossed Highway 111. A mobile home roof was completely removed near the corner of Shell Road and Tri Mile Road along with numerous trees that were either uprooted or snapped. The damage track seemed to end near Snow Bird Lane and Raven Cliff Road. Rear flank downdraft damage was noted farther east along Lewis Chapel Road to about Laurelwood Trail. Major roof damage was seen along Natures Trail and Lewis Chapel Road where winds were estimated around 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moved through the Eastern United States late Tuesday into early Wednesday. The associated surface low pressure system pressed north northeast from the Lower Mississippi across the Mid South on its way across the Lower Ohio Valley. While there were reports of damaging straight line wind with some of the thunderstorms generated ahead of the system cold front, the main and concentrated areas of damage were associated with tornadic supercell thunderstorms. The supercell storms developed in an area of strong shear and sufficient; albeit weak instability. Tornadoes formed in a zone favorable to rotating thunderstorm updrafts just north of a warm frontal boundary across the Tennessee Valley of Alabama northeast through Southeast Tennessee. Three tornadoes developed during the early morning hours on Wednesday, November 30th; impacting McMinn, Polk, Marion, and Sequatchie counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed in far northern Marion County, about one half mile from the Sequatchie County border and quickly intensified to a strong EF-2 tornado with winds of 130 mph as it crossed into Sequatchie County. A double wide mobile home was completely destroyed and an adjacent church roof was heavily damaged. The tornado passed through a grove of trees snapping nearly all of them halfway up the trunk. The strong tornado passed through a farm and caused significant damage to a two story family home. This part of the tornado path was determined to be at the high end of the EF-2 rating. As the tornado continued along a northeasterly track, two additional homes were heavily damaged before moving along an unpopulated ridge. The tornado likely skipped along this ridge as it crossed Highway 127 and then crossed Highway 111. A mobile home roof was completely removed near the corner of Shell Road and Tri Mile Road along with numerous trees that were either uprooted or snapped. The damage track seemed to end near Snow Bird Lane and Raven Cliff Road. Rear flank downdraft damage was noted farther east along Lewis Chapel Road to about Laurelwood Trail. Major roof damage was seen along Natures Trail and Lewis Chapel Road where winds were estimated around 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance triggered storms with very heavy rainfall across portions of west Tennessee on the afternoon of August 2nd. A severe storm also occurred with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant street flooding in Bemis.  Many roadways covered with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front triggered several severe thunderstorms across a large portion of the Midsouth during the late afternoon and early evening hours of July 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail at Apple Tree Center on Mount Moriah Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface cold front and an upper level disturbance provided the focus for a few severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours of July 22nd. Damaging winds and flash flooding were the main threats.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large trees down on Rock Springs Road and Houston Road across the county.
